missions.title: Hello, Container!
missions.story: Welcome, recruit. Your first mission is simple: prove you can launch a container. The docks are quiet, but not for long.
objectives: Run an `nginx` container in the foreground (any name)
hints: The command to run a container is `docker run`
`docker run nginx` — this runs nginx in the foreground

missions.title: Detach and Name
missions.story: Running in the foreground blocks your terminal. Real operators run containers in the background. Name your assets so you can find them later.
objectives: Run an `nginx` container named `webserver` in detached mode
hints: Use flags to detach and name: `-d` and `--name`

missions.title: Inspect the Fleet
missions.story: A good operator always knows the state of their fleet. List all running containers and identify the one named `mission-agent`.
objectives: Run `docker ps` to see running containers (validation auto-passes after you run `docker ps` once)
Extra: Find the container ID of `mission-agent` and run `docker inspect mission-agent`

missions.title: Port Mapping
missions.story: The web server is running but no one outside can reach it. Map port 8080 on the host to port 80 inside the container.
objectives: Run an `nginx` container named `webserver` mapping host port 8080 to container port 80, in detached mode
hints: Use `-p` flag for port mapping: `-p host:container`

missions.title: Environment Variables
missions.story: Applications need configuration. Pass secrets and settings through environment variables — never hardcode them.
objectives: Run a container from image `alpine` named `env-demo` with env var `APP_ENV=production` and `APP_VERSION=1.0`, keep it running (use `sleep 3600`)
hints: Use `-e` flag to set environment variables
`docker run -d --name env-demo -e APP_ENV=production -e APP_VERSION=1.0 alpine sleep 3600`
`docker run -d --name env-demo -e APP_ENV=production -e APP_VERSION=1.0 alpine sleep 3600`

missions.title: Stop and Remove
missions.story: Containers left running waste resources. Stop and remove the container named `old-service` to clean up the docks.
objectives: Stop the container named `old-service`
Remove the container named `old-service`
hints: `docker stop <name>` stops a container; `docker rm <name>` removes it
Stop first, then remove: `docker stop old-service && docker rm old-service`
Or force remove in one step: `docker rm -f old-service`

missions.title: Exec Into a Container
missions.story: Something is wrong inside the `debug-box` container. Get a shell inside it and create a file `/tmp/mission-complete` to signal success.
objectives: Exec into container `debug-box` and create file `/tmp/mission-complete`
hints: `docker exec -it <name> <command>` runs a command inside a running container
Use `docker exec debug-box touch /tmp/mission-complete` or exec a shell

missions.title: Container Logs
missions.story: The `log-service` container is misbehaving. Read its logs to find the secret code it printed, then create a file `/tmp/found` inside it to confirm you found it.
objectives: Read the logs of `log-service`
Create file `/tmp/found` inside `log-service`
hints: `docker logs <name>` prints the stdout/stderr of a container
After reading logs, exec in and create the file
`docker logs log-service` then `docker exec log-service touch /tmp/found`

initialState.summary: - Container `log-service` (alpine, running) pre-created, it ran: `echo "SECRET_CODE=DOCKER42"` then `sleep 3600`

missions.title: Pull an Image
missions.story: Before you can deploy, you need the right image. Pull `redis:7-alpine` from Docker Hub — lightweight and battle-tested.
objectives: Pull image `redis:7-alpine` from Docker Hub

missions.title: List and Remove Images
missions.story: The local image store is cluttered with old images consuming disk space. Remove the image `busybox:latest` to free space.
objectives: Remove image `busybox:latest`
hints: `docker images` lists local images; `docker rmi <image>` removes one

missions.title: Your First Dockerfile
missions.story: It's time to build something. Create a Dockerfile that starts from `alpine:3.18`, sets maintainer label `maintainer=recruit`, and runs `echo "Mission Ready"` as the default command.
objectives: Write a `Dockerfile` in `/workspace`
Build it as image `mission-image:v1`
hints: A Dockerfile starts with `FROM`, then `LABEL`, then `CMD`
Write the Dockerfile above, then `docker build -t mission-image:v1 /workspace`

missions.title: ADD vs COPY
missions.story: Your app needs its source code inside the image. Use COPY to add `app.py` from the build context into `/app/app.py` in the image.
objectives: Write a Dockerfile using `FROM python:3.11-alpine`, `COPY app.py /app/app.py`, `CMD ["python", "/app/app.py"]`
Build as `pyapp:v1`
hints: `COPY <src> <dest>` copies files from build context to image
Dockerfile with FROM, COPY, CMD; build with `docker build -t pyapp:v1 /workspace`
Write the Dockerfile, then `docker build -t pyapp:v1 /workspace`

missions.title: RUN Commands
missions.story: A bare OS image needs software installed. Build an image based on `ubuntu:22.04` that has `curl` installed.
objectives: Write a Dockerfile: `FROM ubuntu:22.04`, `RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y curl`
Build as `curl-box:v1`
Verify: run a container from `curl-box:v1` and confirm `curl --version` works
hints: Chain commands with `&&` to minimize layers

missions.title: ENV and WORKDIR
missions.story: Well-structured images define their working directory and bake in environment configuration. Build an image with `WORKDIR /app` and `ENV NODE_ENV=production`.
objectives: Write a Dockerfile: `FROM node:18-alpine`, `WORKDIR /app`, `ENV NODE_ENV=production`, `CMD ["node", "-e", "console.log(process.env.NODE_ENV)"]`
Build as `node-app:v1`
hints: `WORKDIR` sets the working directory; `ENV` sets environment variables
Both are Dockerfile instructions, one per line
Write the Dockerfile with all four instructions, then build

missions.title: Multi-Stage Build
missions.story: Bloated images are a security risk and waste bandwidth. Use a multi-stage build to compile a Go binary in one stage and copy only the binary into a minimal `scratch` or `alpine` final image.
objectives: Write a multi-stage Dockerfile:
Stage 1 (`builder`): `FROM golang:1.21-alpine AS builder`, copy `main.go`, run `go build -o app .`
Stage 2: `FROM alpine:3.18`, `COPY --from=builder /build/app /app`, `CMD ["/app"]`
Build as `go-app:v1`
Final image size should be under 20MB
hints: Multi-stage builds use multiple `FROM` lines; `COPY --from=<stage>` copies between stages
The builder stage has all dev tools; the final stage is lean
See the multi-stage Dockerfile structure in the objectives

initialState.summary: `/workspace/main.go` pre-created (a simple Hello World Go program)

missions.title: .dockerignore
missions.story: Your build context is huge because it includes `node_modules` and `.git`. Create a `.dockerignore` file to exclude them and speed up builds.
objectives: Create `/workspace/.dockerignore` that excludes `node_modules` and `.git`
Build image `clean-build:v1` from the existing Dockerfile in `/workspace`
Verify the build context sent to daemon is under 1KB
hints: `.dockerignore` works like `.gitignore` — list patterns to exclude from build context
Create `.dockerignore` with those two entries, then `docker build -t clean-build:v1 /workspace`

initialState.summary: `/workspace` contains a Dockerfile, a small `index.js`, large `node_modules/` (fake, 100MB), `.git/`

missions.title: Default Bridge Network
missions.story: By default, containers join the `bridge` network. Run two alpine containers and observe that they can't resolve each other by name on the default bridge.
objectives: Run container `alpha` from `alpine` (detached, `sleep 3600`)
Run container `beta` from `alpine` (detached, `sleep 3600`)
Try to ping `beta` from `alpha` — observe it fails (expected)
Confirm both containers are on the `bridge` network
hints: Default containers use the `bridge` network automatically
DNS-based name resolution only works on user-defined networks

missions.title: Create a Custom Network
missions.story: User-defined networks give you DNS resolution between containers. Create a network named `mission-net` of type `bridge`.
objectives: Create a Docker network named `mission-net` with driver `bridge`
hints: Use `--driver bridge` (bridge is the default anyway)

missions.title: Connect Containers by Name
missions.story: Now that you have `mission-net`, connect two containers to it. They should be able to ping each other by name.
objectives: Run container `server` from `nginx` (detached) on network `mission-net`
Run container `client` from `alpine` (detached, `sleep 3600`) on network `mission-net`
Exec into `client` and ping `server` by name (must succeed)
hints: `docker exec client ping -c 1 server` should succeed

missions.title: Expose vs Publish
missions.story: `EXPOSE` in a Dockerfile is documentation only — it doesn't publish the port to the host. You must use `-p` to actually publish. Run nginx publishing port 9090 on the host.
objectives: Run `nginx` container named `public-web` with host port `9090` mapped to container port `80`
Confirm the port is reachable on `localhost:9090`

missions.title: Host Network
missions.story: For maximum performance (no NAT), some containers run on the host network. Run a container using the `host` network driver and confirm it sees the host's network interfaces.
objectives: Run container `host-mode` from `alpine` using `--network host` running `sleep 3600` (detached)
Exec into it and run `ip addr` — confirm `eth0` or host interface is visible
hints: `--network host` attaches container directly to host network
No port mapping needed or possible with host mode

missions.title: Connect Running Container to Network
missions.story: The container `late-joiner` is already running but needs to join `mission-net` without restarting. Connect it dynamically.
objectives: Connect running container `late-joiner` to network `mission-net` without stopping it
hints: `docker network connect <network> <container>` attaches a running container

initialState.summary: - Container `late-joiner` (alpine, running, `sleep 3600`) on default bridge - Network `mission-net` exists

missions.title: Isolate with None Network
missions.story: Some workloads must be completely isolated with no network access. Run a container with no networking at all and confirm it cannot reach the outside world.
objectives: Run container `isolated` from `alpine` with no network (`--network none`) running `sleep 3600`
Confirm it has no network interfaces except `lo`
hints: The container will only have the loopback `lo` interface

missions.title: Named Volume Basics
missions.story: Containers are ephemeral — when they die, their data dies too. Named volumes live beyond containers. Create a volume named `app-data`.
objectives: Create a Docker named volume called `app-data`
hints: `docker volume create <name>` creates a named volume

missions.title: Mount a Volume
missions.story: A volume is only useful when mounted. Run an `alpine` container named `writer` with volume `app-data` mounted at `/data`, then write a file to it.
objectives: Run container `writer` from `alpine` with `app-data` mounted at `/data` (detached, `sleep 3600`)
Create file `/data/record.txt` inside the container with content `mission=complete`
hints: Use `-v <volume>:<path>` or `--mount` to attach a volume

missions.title: Data Survives Container Death
missions.story: Prove that data in a volume outlives its container. The `writer` container has been removed. Run a new container `reader` on the same volume and confirm the file still exists.
objectives: Run container `reader` from `alpine` mounting `app-data` at `/data`
Read `/data/record.txt` and confirm content is `mission=complete`
hints: Mount the same volume in a new container

initialState.summary: - Volume `app-data` exists with `/data/record.txt` containing `mission=complete` - Container `writer` has been removed

missions.title: Bind Mounts
missions.story: Bind mounts link a host directory directly into a container — perfect for development. Mount the host directory `/workspace/html` into an nginx container at `/usr/share/nginx/html`.
objectives: Run `nginx` container named `dev-web` with host path `/workspace/html` bind-mounted at `/usr/share/nginx/html`, publishing port 8080
hints: Bind mounts use absolute host paths

missions.title: tmpfs Mount
missions.story: Some sensitive data (tokens, secrets) should never touch disk. Use a `tmpfs` mount for in-memory-only storage.
objectives: Run container `secure-app` from `alpine` with a `tmpfs` mount at `/secrets` (detached, `sleep 3600`)
Write a file `/secrets/token.txt` to it
Confirm the data is in memory (not on a volume or bind mount)
hints: Data in tmpfs is lost when the container stops

missions.title: Volume Backup
missions.story: Volumes must be backed up. Use a temporary container to tar the contents of `app-data` volume to `/backup/app-data.tar.gz` on the host.
objectives: Run a temporary container that mounts `app-data` at `/data` and `/backup` as a bind mount
Compress `/data` contents into `/backup/app-data.tar.gz`
Container should stop after completing the backup
hints: Mount both the volume and the backup directory

initialState.summary: - Volume `app-data` with files pre-created - Host directory `/backup` exists and is writable

missions.title: Your First Compose File
missions.story: Running multi-container apps with `docker run` is painful. Docker Compose lets you define everything in one YAML file. Write a Compose file that runs a single `nginx` service.
objectives: Write `/workspace/docker-compose.yml` with one service: `web` using image `nginx:latest`, no port mapping yet
Run `docker compose up -d`
Confirm service `web` is running
hints: A Compose file has a `services:` key at the top level
Write the file, then `docker compose -f /workspace/docker-compose.yml up -d`

missions.title: Multiple Services
missions.story: Real apps have multiple components. Define a Compose stack with a `web` (nginx) service and a `db` (postgres:15-alpine) service.
objectives: Write a `docker-compose.yml` with services `web` (nginx) and `db` (postgres:15-alpine)
Set required env var for postgres: `POSTGRES_PASSWORD=secret`
Run `docker compose up -d`

missions.title: Port Mapping in Compose
missions.story: Expose the nginx service on host port 8080.
objectives: Add `ports: ["8080:80"]` to the `web` service
Restart the stack
Confirm http://localhost:8080 responds

missions.title: Named Volumes in Compose
missions.story: Your Postgres data disappears on every `docker compose down`. Add a named volume `db-data` to persist it.
objectives: Add a named volume `db-data` to the Compose file
Mount it in the `db` service at `/var/lib/postgresql/data`
Re-deploy the stack
hints: Define volumes at the top level `volumes:` and reference them in services
Add both the top-level `volumes:` block and the service-level mount

missions.title: Custom Network in Compose
missions.story: By default Compose creates one network. Define a custom network `backend` and put the `db` on it. Put `web` on both `backend` and a `frontend` network.
objectives: Define two networks: `frontend` and `backend`
`web` service on both networks
`db` service only on `backend` network
hints: Define networks at the top level `networks:` and reference under each service
A service can list multiple networks
Use `networks:` key both at the top level and under each service

missions.title: Health Checks and Depends_on
missions.story: The `web` service crashes on startup because `db` isn't ready. Use `depends_on` with a health check to make `web` wait for `db` to be healthy before starting.
objectives: Add a `healthcheck` to the `db` service: `pg_isready -U postgres`
Add `depends_on: db: condition: service_healthy` to the `web` service
Redeploy and confirm `web` starts only after `db` is healthy

missions.title: Scale a Service
missions.story: Traffic is spiking. Scale the `web` service to 3 replicas using Docker Compose.
objectives: Scale the `web` service to 3 replicas
Confirm 3 containers for the `web` service are running
hints: `docker compose up --scale web=3 -d` scales a service
Or add `deploy.replicas: 3` in the Compose file (requires Swarm or Compose v3 with deploy)

missions.title: Run as Non-Root
missions.story: Containers running as root are dangerous. If they escape, they own your host. Run an alpine container as user ID `1000` instead of root.
objectives: Run container `safe-app` from `alpine` as user `1000` (`sleep 3600`, detached)
Confirm the container process does NOT run as root
hints: Use `--user <uid>` to set the container user

missions.title: Read-Only Filesystem
missions.story: A container that can't write to its own filesystem is significantly harder to compromise. Run nginx in read-only mode.
objectives: Run `nginx` container named `readonly-web` with a read-only filesystem (`--read-only`)
Nginx needs a writable tmpfs at `/tmp` and `/var/run` to start — add those
Publish port 8080
hints: Combine with `--tmpfs` for directories that must be writable

missions.title: Drop Linux Capabilities
missions.story: Containers inherit too many Linux capabilities by default. Drop ALL capabilities from a container and add back only what's needed.
objectives: Run container `minimal-caps` from `alpine` dropping ALL capabilities with `--cap-drop=ALL`
Add back only `NET_BIND_SERVICE` with `--cap-add`
Run `sleep 3600` detached

missions.title: Seccomp Profile
missions.story: Seccomp filters which system calls a container can make. Apply the default Docker seccomp profile explicitly to lock down `syscall-test` container.
objectives: Run container `syscall-test` from `alpine` with the default seccomp profile applied explicitly
Run `sleep 3600` detached
hints: The profile is JSON file defining allowed syscalls

missions.title: Resource Limits
missions.story: A runaway container can starve the host. Limit container `resource-hog` to 256MB RAM and 0.5 CPU.
objectives: Run container `resource-hog` from `alpine` with:
Memory limit: `256m`
CPU limit: `0.5`
Run `sleep 3600` detached
hints: `--memory 256m` sets RAM limit; `--cpus 0.5` sets CPU limit
These prevent the container from consuming unlimited host resources

missions.title: Scan an Image for Vulnerabilities
missions.story: You've built an image `legacy-app:v1`. Use Docker Scout or Trivy to scan it for critical CVEs, then rebuild using an updated base image to eliminate the critical vulnerabilities.
objectives: Scan `legacy-app:v1` (pre-built with old ubuntu base) and identify critical vulnerabilities
Rebuild with updated base image `ubuntu:22.04` as `legacy-app:v2`
Scan `legacy-app:v2` and confirm zero critical CVEs
hints: `docker scout cves legacy-app:v1` or `trivy image legacy-app:v1`
Change `FROM ubuntu:18.04` to `FROM ubuntu:22.04` in the Dockerfile

initialState.summary: - `legacy-app:v1` pre-built using `ubuntu:18.04` (has known CVEs) - `/workspace/Dockerfile` available for editing

workspaceFiles.content:
FROM ubuntu:18.04
RUN apt-get update
RUN apt-get install -y curl
RUN apt-get install -y wget
CMD ["bash", "-lc", "sleep 3600"]

missions.title: Tag an Image
missions.story: Before pushing to a registry, images must be tagged with the destination. Tag the local image `myapp:latest` to target the local registry at `localhost:5000/myapp:v1`.
objectives: Tag `myapp:latest` as `localhost:5000/myapp:v1`
hints: `docker tag <source> <destination>` creates a new tag pointing to the same image

missions.title: Run a Local Registry
missions.story: You don't always need Docker Hub. Run a local Docker registry on port 5000.
objectives: Run a local registry container named `registry` from `registry:2` on port 5000 (detached)
hints: The official `registry:2` image runs a Docker-compatible registry

missions.title: Push to Local Registry
missions.story: Your local registry is running. Push `localhost:5000/myapp:v1` to it.
objectives: Push the image `localhost:5000/myapp:v1` to the local registry
hints: `docker push <image>` pushes to the registry encoded in the image name

initialState.summary: - Local registry running on port 5000 - `localhost:5000/myapp:v1` image tagged locally

workspaceFiles.content:
FROM ubuntu:22.04
RUN apt-get update
RUN apt-get install -y curl
RUN apt-get install -y wget
RUN apt-get install -y git
CMD ["bash", "-lc", "echo mission ready"]

missions.title: Pull from Local Registry
missions.story: The image was pushed. Now remove the local copy and re-pull it from the registry to prove the registry works.
objectives: Remove local image `localhost:5000/myapp:v1`
Pull it back from the registry
Run a container from it to verify
hints: `docker rmi` removes local image; `docker pull` re-downloads

missions.title: Image Layers and History
missions.story: Every Dockerfile instruction adds a layer. Understanding layers helps you optimize images. Inspect the layer history of `myapp:v1` and rebuild with optimized layers (fewer RUN commands merged).
objectives: Run `docker history myapp:v1` to see all layers
Identify layers that can be merged (multiple consecutive `RUN` commands)
Edit `/workspace/Dockerfile` to merge the RUN commands
Build `myapp:v2` and confirm it has fewer layers than `myapp:v1`
hints: `docker history <image>` shows all layers and their sizes
Merge multiple `RUN` commands with `&&` to reduce layer count

initialState.summary: - `myapp:v1` pre-built with at least 3 separate `RUN apt-get ...` instructions - `/workspace/Dockerfile` editable

workspaceFiles.content:
FROM ubuntu:22.04
RUN apt-get update
RUN apt-get install -y curl
RUN apt-get install -y wget
RUN apt-get install -y git
CMD ["bash", "-lc", "echo mission ready"]

missions.title: BuildKit and Build Cache
missions.story: Docker BuildKit dramatically speeds up builds using intelligent caching and parallel execution. Enable BuildKit and rebuild an existing image to see cache hits.
objectives: Set `DOCKER_BUILDKIT=1` environment variable
Build `cached-app:v1` from `/workspace/Dockerfile`
Modify only `index.html` (not the Dockerfile) and rebuild as `cached-app:v2`
Confirm the second build uses cache for unchanged layers
hints: Or set in Docker daemon config: `"features": {"buildkit": true}`

missions.title: Cache Mount in BuildKit
missions.story: Package manager downloads are slow and happen on every build. Use BuildKit's `--mount=type=cache` to cache `apt` downloads across builds.
objectives: Edit `/workspace/Dockerfile` to use `RUN --mount=type=cache,target=/var/cache/apt apt-get install ...`
Build `cache-mount:v1` and then `cache-mount:v2` (same Dockerfile, second build should be faster)

workspaceFiles.content:
FROM ubuntu:22.04
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y curl

missions.title: Docker Init
missions.story: PID 1 in a container must properly handle signals and reap zombie processes. Use `--init` to run an init process as PID 1.
objectives: Run container `signal-safe` from `node:18-alpine` with `--init` flag
Confirm PID 1 is `docker-init` (tini), not the Node process directly
hints: `--init` injects a tiny init process (tini) as PID 1
The actual application becomes a child of PID 1

missions.title: Docker Context
missions.story: Docker contexts let you switch between different Docker daemons (local, remote, production). Create a context named `production` pointing to a remote Docker socket.
objectives: Create a Docker context named `production` with host `ssh://deploy@10.0.0.100`
List contexts to confirm it exists
Switch back to `default` context after inspection

missions.title: Healthcheck in Dockerfile
missions.story: Docker can monitor your container's health and restart it if it becomes unhealthy. Add a HEALTHCHECK to a Dockerfile for an HTTP service.
objectives: Edit `/workspace/Dockerfile` (nginx-based) to add:
Build as `healthy-nginx:v1`
Run a container and confirm it reaches `healthy` status
hints: The CMD must return 0 for healthy, 1 for unhealthy
Wait ~30 seconds after starting for the health status to be `healthy`

missions.title: Docker Swarm Init
missions.story: Docker Swarm turns your Docker host into a cluster manager. Initialize a single-node Swarm.
objectives: Initialize Docker Swarm with `docker swarm init`
Deploy a service named `web` with 3 replicas using `nginx` image
Confirm all 3 replicas are running
hints: `docker service ls` shows services; `docker service ps web` shows tasks

missions.title: Prune Everything
missions.story: Your Docker host is running out of disk space. Clean up all stopped containers, unused images, unused networks, and dangling volumes in one sweep.
objectives: Remove all stopped containers
Remove all unused images (not just dangling)
Remove all unused networks
Remove all unused volumes
Confirm disk space is recovered
hints: `docker system prune -a --volumes` cleans everything at once (with `-a` for all images)
Or run individually: `docker container prune`, `docker image prune -a`, `docker volume prune`, `docker network prune`

initialState.summary: Many stopped containers, unused images, and dangling volumes pre-created
